skill.name: lark-slides
skill.description: Create, read, and manage Lark Slides presentations through lark-cli using the Slides XML format.
skill.summary: Build and update Lark Slides decks with XML templates, media upload guidance, and Lark CLI workflows.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, filesystem, weak-crypto, and entropy hits. Manual review found no prompt-injection attempt and no confirmed malicious intent; most high-risk hits are markdown/XML false positives, while the real risk is intentional lark-cli execution, authenticated Lark network calls, local image uploads, and irreversible slide deletion.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Lark CLI Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run lark-cli commands for authentication, presentation creation, slide creation, reading, and deletion. This is expected functionality, but it can change cloud documents and should run only with user intent and visible parameters.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple documented command examples confirm intentional execution. The commands target Lark Slides workflows, so the risk is operational rather than hidden malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload to Lark Media Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill supports uploading local images from the current working directory to Lark media storage and replacing XML image sources with returned file tokens. This can expose local files if the assistant selects the wrong path or if the user does not review uploads.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The docs explicitly describe local path placeholders, media upload commands, and Lark file tokens. They also document path restrictions, which lowers but does not remove upload risk.

medium_findings.title: Irreversible Slide Deletion Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill includes workflows for deleting slide pages and replacing pages by creating a new page before deleting the old one. This is legitimate but can cause data loss if slide IDs or ordering are wrong.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deletion commands and warnings are explicit. The skill includes safeguards, but the operation remains destructive when executed against a real presentation.

low_findings.title: Static Path Traversal Findings Are Guardrail Examples
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged parent-directory and absolute-path strings, but the reviewed lines describe paths that the CLI rejects as unsafe. This is a false positive for malicious path traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant text is a safety rule, not code that reads arbitrary paths. It explicitly says absolute and parent-directory paths are rejected.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs are used as XML namespaces, placeholder Lark presentation links, and examples showing why external image URLs should not be embedded directly. No evidence of unauthorized external exfiltration was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are protocol namespaces or placeholder examples tied to documented Lark workflows. Network access still exists through lark-cli, but these specific static hits are not malicious endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Obfuscation Hits Are XML and Template False Positives
low_findings.description: The high weak-crypto and entropy findings map to XML schema values, template text, media token examples, and dense multilingual documentation. No cryptographic implementation, encoded payload, or decoder chain was identified in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations contain shape names, image path guidance, and token placeholders rather than crypto calls. This strongly supports false positive classification for the high static category.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in CLI Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples use command substitution with jq and lark-cli output to build request payloads. This is useful for structured CLI calls, but it would be unsafe if untrusted text were inserted into shell commands without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is clearly present in bash examples. The examples are legitimate, but shell interpolation remains a known dangerous pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Path Media Placeholder Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The @ path placeholder causes local files to be read and uploaded during slide creation. The docs restrict paths to the current working directory, but the pattern still needs user confirmation before upload.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder behavior is explicitly documented and tied to media upload. The documented path guardrails make the risk moderate rather than high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Document-Modifying and Delete Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands that create, insert, and delete cloud-hosted slide pages. These operations should be treated as state-changing actions that require user intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly modify cloud presentation state. The documents provide warnings, but the pattern is still dangerous if used on the wrong presentation.

content.value_statement: Creating polished Lark Slides decks requires exact XML and command syntax. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through reliable slide creation, media upload, reading, and page management workflows.
actual_capabilities: Creates new Lark Slides presentations with lark-cli shortcut commands.
Generates slide XML using the included schema quick reference and templates.
Reads existing presentation XML for review or page replacement workflows.
Adds new slides to existing presentations with placement guidance.
Uploads local images to Lark media storage and uses returned file tokens.
Deletes slide pages with documented safeguards and required identifiers.
limitations: Requires lark-cli, Lark authentication, and the correct Slides API permissions.
Does not provide element-level editing; existing slides must be replaced as whole pages.
Image uploads are limited to local relative paths and the documented media size limit.
External image URLs must be downloaded and uploaded before use in slide XML.

use_cases.title: Create a business review deck
use_cases.description: Turn a confirmed outline into a Lark Slides presentation with consistent layouts, colors, and page structure.

use_cases.title: Add visual pages to an existing deck
use_cases.description: Upload approved local images, create new image slides, and keep the presentation order under control.

use_cases.title: Inspect and repair slide XML
use_cases.description: Read presentation XML, find formatting issues, and rebuild affected pages using schema-backed XML rules.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simple deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Lark Slides deck about [topic] for [audience]. Use [page count] pages, a professional style, and ask me to confirm the outline before creating it.

prompt_templates.title: Build from an outline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this approved outline to create a Lark Slides presentation. Keep each slide focused, use the schema reference, and return the presentation link and ID when complete.

prompt_templates.title: Add image slides
prompt_templates.prompt: Add these local images to my existing Lark Slides presentation. Upload each file, use file tokens in slide XML, and place the new slides after the relevant section.

prompt_templates.title: Replace an existing page
prompt_templates.prompt: Read this presentation, replace slide [slide ID] with a revised page, preserve all existing useful content, create the new page before deleting the old one, and explain the changed slide ID.

output_examples.input: Create a five-page quarterly review deck for executives.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a five-page outline, confirms the business style, creates the deck, then returns the presentation title, ID, and access link.

output_examples.input: Add a chart image to an existing Lark Slides presentation.
output_examples.output: The assistant uploads the local image, creates a new slide that references the media token, and reports the new slide position.

output_examples.input: Fix a slide where the image covered the text.
output_examples.output: The assistant reads the current XML, rebuilds the page with safer coordinates, inserts the replacement, and deletes the old page after success.

best_practices: Confirm the outline, audience, page count, and visual style before running creation commands.
Use user identity by default and request bot identity only when the user explicitly needs it.
Keep image files in the current working directory and use relative paths for uploads.
anti_patterns: Do not delete an existing slide before the replacement slide is created successfully.
Do not place external HTTP image URLs directly in slide XML.
Do not guess XML elements or command parameters without checking the included schema guidance.

faq.question: Does this skill create Lark Slides presentations?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses lark-cli commands and Slides XML to create presentations and add pages.

faq.question: Can it edit one element on an existing slide?
faq.answer: No. The documented API supports slide-level creation and deletion, so page replacement is required.

faq.question: Can it use local images?
faq.answer: Yes. Local images must use relative paths and must be uploaded before they appear in slide XML.

faq.question: Can it use image URLs directly?
faq.answer: No. External image URLs should be downloaded first, then uploaded through the Lark media workflow.

faq.question: What permissions are needed?
faq.answer: The user needs Lark authentication and relevant Slides or media upload scopes for the requested action.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings because it intentionally runs lark-cli commands and can upload local files.
